Is your growth strategy ready for the "AI-first" search era?
Jooble Country Head USA, Yevhen Onatsko, recently joined Ben Geoghegan on the Podcast: A Better HR Business for a deep dive into the mechanics of scaling a global tech company.
If you’re an HR consultant, a recruiter, or a business leader, this conversation is less about Jooble’s history and more about the future of your industry.

Yevhen and Ben discussed the high-level tactics that are working right now in 2026:
• Beyond the SEO Bubble: How search behaviors are shifting with LLMs/ChatGPT and how to adapt your content so you don't disappear.
• The Consultant’s Playbook: Practical steps to find high-value clients and win international contracts through credibility, not just cold outreach.
• Market Intelligence: Why "vanity metrics" are dead and how to use deep data to identify hiring gaps before your competitors do.
• Trust at Scale: The exact partnership strategies we used to break into the US market.

Whether you're looking to scale your own consultancy or just want to understand how AI is reshaping the recruitment landscape, this episode is packed with "meat."

🎧 Listen to the full conversation here: https://lnkd.in/dBftBr2E

Hiring philosophy in AI era! 

With Agentic AI being the future and the delivery becoming outcome based, almost all IT jobs will become outcome based where AI agents will be mainstream employees and humans delivering projects / roles / responsibilities will be only relevant to defined outcome post which their contracts will end. 

This will be the case top to bottom. Eg: we already see fragmented CXOs.

Everyone’s hiring agents now.

Not people. 

Software. 

Systems that negotiate, approve, execute, and decide on your behalf while you sleep. 

The pitch sounds like freedom. 

The reality is that nobody architected the accountability layer before they hit deploy.

This is the part that fascinates me.

We’d never hand a new hire the company card, the client list, and the authority to sign contracts on day one with zero oversight. But we’re doing exactly that with autonomous systems, and calling it innovation.

The consent infrastructure underneath most AI deployments was designed for a human clicking a box in 2012. 

It was never built for an agent accepting terms at scale, processing sensitive data across jurisdictions, making micro-decisions that compound before anyone notices.

And compounding is the word. 

Not the venture capital kind. 

The kind where a small ungoverned action repeats a thousand times before Tuesday, and by the time someone flags it, you’re not dealing with a bug. You’re dealing with a pattern.

The founders I advise aren’t asking “should we adopt AI agents?” 

That question is already answered. 

They’re asking the one that actually matters: can we prove, under pressure, how this system makes decisions, who authorised the delegation, and what happens when it’s wrong?

That’s not a compliance exercise. That’s a leadership posture.
And right now, the market is quietly bifurcating. 

Companies that can answer those questions command premium. 
Companies that can’t are one incident away from explaining themselves in a room they didn’t prepare for.

Governance isn’t the thing that slows you down.

It’s the thing that means you’re still in the room when the room gets serious.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐓𝐲𝐩𝐞𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐑𝐞𝐜𝐫𝐮𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬:  A Complete Professional Breakdown
Recruitment is not just about hiring - it's about #strategy, #specialization, and #talent_intelligence.
Different recruiters play different roles depending on #hiring_needs, #industry, #urgency, and #hiring_level.

Understanding recruiter types helps organizations:
*Improve hiring strategy
*Reduce time-to-hire
*Improve candidate quality
*Build strong talent pipelines
*Scale hiring effectively

1.#Internal_Recruiter
An Internal Recruiter works directly within a company and handles hiring exclusively for that organization.
Key Responsibilities
-Manage end-to-end recruitment
-Work with hiring managers
-Post job openings
-Screen candidates
-Coordinate interviews
-Offer negotiation

2.#External_Recruiter 
External recruiters work for recruitment agencies and hire candidates for multiple companies.
Key Responsibilities
-Source candidates
-Client management
-Headhunting
-Market intelligence
-Fast hiring

3.#Headhunter 
Headhunters focus on passive candidates - professionals who are currently employed and not actively looking.
Key Responsibilities
-Market mapping
-Competitor talent search
-Passive candidate outreach
-Confidential hiring

4.#Corporate_Recruiter
Corporate recruiters manage hiring within large organizations across departments.
Key Responsibilities
-Multiple department hiring
-Stakeholder management
-Workforce planning
-Hiring strategy

5.#Technical_Recruiter
Technical recruiters specialize in hiring IT and technical professionals.
Roles They Hire
-Software Engineers
-Developers
-DevOps Engineers
-Data Scientists
-AI Engineers

6.#Non_Technical_Recruiter
Non-technical recruiters focus on business and operational roles.
Roles They Hire
-HR
-Finance
-Marketing
-Sales

7.#Executive_Recruiter
Executive recruiters hire senior leadership roles.
Roles They Hire
-CEO-CFO-CTO-VP-Directors

8.#Contract_Recruiter
Contract recruiters are hired temporarily to support recruitment projects.
Best For
-Mass hiring
-Seasonal hiring
-Project hiring

9. #Campus_Recruiter
Campus recruiters hire fresh graduates from universities.
Responsibilities
-University partnerships
-Career fairs
-Internship hiring

10.#Sourcing_Recruiter 
Sourcing recruiters focus only on finding candidates.
Responsibilities
-Boolean search
-LinkedIn sourcing
-Talent mapping
-Pipeline building

11.#High_Volume_Recruiter
High-volume recruiters manage mass hiring.

12.#RPO_Recruiter
RPO recruiters work for outsourcing companies and manage recruitment for clients.

13.#Freelance_Recruiter
Independent recruiters working on commission or contract basis.

14.#Diversity_Recruiter
Focuses on diversity and inclusion hiring.

15.#Talent_Acquisition_Specialist
Strategic recruiter focused on long-term hiring planning.


#Recruiter_Career_Progression
Recruiter → Senior Recruiter → Lead Recruiter → Talent Partner → Talent Acquisition Manager → Head of Talent → HR Director

The cost of a wrong hire hasn’t changed. But the chances of making one just increased.

Most roles get flooded with applications. A corporate job can receive 250+ applications on average. Only 2–3% of applicants typically get interviewed based on the resume submitted. AI was meant to help filter, but instead, it amplified the noise. Because AI doesn’t change what we measure, it just speeds it up.

And what we’re still measuring is:
- how well someone can talk about their experience
- how polished their answers sound
- how convincingly they present themselves

Not whether they can actually do the job.

That gap has always existed. But AI has made it more visible and in some cases, worse.

Because now:
- the cost of applying is close to zero 
- candidates are using AI to optimise how they present
- screening systems are trained on already flawed signals

So we end up with a system that increasingly rewards presentation over capability.

So what do you do instead? Here are a few things that actually work in practice:

1) Build in a probation/trial mindset earlier 

 Instead of treating hiring as a one-off decision, treat it as a process of validation by using:
 – short-term projects at the start where possible
 – contract-to-perm structures
 – clearly defined success criteria in the first 30–60 days

2) assess how people think, not what they say

- Don't ask about achievements listed on the resume, read between the lines and question what's not stated explicitly
- Challenge their decisions
- Give incomplete or messy information and see how they navigate it
- keep digging with follow-up questions until all details are fully clarified

3) reduce reliance on CVs as the primary filter, use it as a context tool instead. 
Resumes have become marketing documents. Useful, but no longer reliable as a signal on their own.

Try introducing a short task/scenario that they need to think through (this has always been used at assessment centres to identify top-talent).

4) Train hiring managers to separate signal from polish and make sure everyone is on board. 

So this question is now more important than ever: “How do we actually test whether someone can do the job — before we make a decision?”

Because until that changes, we’re just getting better at selecting the best storytellers. Not the best people.

7 ways Project Glasswing will change how I ship AI products.

Not a single one requires me to stop building - it just makes the foundation safer.

1. Code audits - Glasswing's focus makes security-first reviews non-negotiable for model-serving stacks.
   Saved me from a late-night panic when a third-party lib introduced a vulnerability.

2. Dependency management - Thinking about critical software supply chains is suddenly mainstream.
   I no longer push updates blind; I triage risks before they become incidents.

3. Model validation - Threat modelling for models is now part of the release checklist.
   Stopped one rollout that would have exposed sensitive predictions to adversarial inputs.

4. Incident response - With clearer playbooks, post-mortems go from finger-pointing to fixes.
   Reduced debugging time by days when we could follow a reproducible threat hypothesis.

5. Compliance and customer trust - Security focus gives sales and legal something concrete to point to.
   Clients now ask fewer last-minute questions and sign production contracts faster.

6. Hiring and culture - Security becomes a team KPI, not just a checkbox for infra teams.
   New hires onboard faster because the expectations for safe design are documented.

7. Product roadmaps - We prioritise hardening critical paths over feature bloat.
   Fewer hotfixes, more predictable sprints, and happier customers in regulated industries.

Anthropic launching Project Glasswing is a reminder: as AI features multiply, the real pressure will be on the reliability of the software under them. For Indian developers and startups, that means investing in secure foundations now so you can scale confidently later.

Read that again. Wipro (WIPRO) didn't just win a contract. It acquired Mindsprint — Olam's in-house IT arm with 3,200 employees and $135M in revenue — so it could walk in on day one already knowing every system, every process, every file.
Zero ramp-up time. Instant domain expertise in food and agri supply chains. $800M in committed spend.
Why does this matter beyond Wipro?
Indian IT organic growth is crawling at 3-4%. Wipro's own IT services segment revenue grew just 0.2% YoY last quarter. When you can't grow organically, you acquire.
And the industry is doing exactly that. IT companies are expected to spend $6.5-7B on acquisitions in FY27, up from $5B last year. Buying client captives — their in-house IT teams — is becoming the new growth playbook. Especially when the old model of billing clients per engineer is under pressure as AI automates routine work. Deals like this flip the script — instead of being a vendor that AI can replace, you become the one deploying AI inside the client's operations.
The logic is sharp: you get a trained team, embedded client relationships, domain IP, and long-term revenue locked in one move. You're not selling hours anymore. You're owning outcomes.
The risk is equally sharp: Mindsprint's revenue is heavily concentrated on one client. If Olam faces headwinds, Wipro's $375M bet gets uncomfortable.
For IT professionals in India — this deal signals that understanding a client's business deeply matters more than ever. Domain expertise in verticals like agriculture, supply chain, and trading is where hiring premiums are moving.
Can Wipro turn a single-client captive into a multi-client platform? That decides whether this is a masterstroke or an expensive gamble.

The engineers companies laid off in 2023 didn't disappear. They're just more expensive now.

We're seeing it firsthand: companies that cut aggressively two years ago are re-hiring for nearly identical roles — but budgets have had to reset upward 15–25% to compete. The talent pool shrank. The ones who stayed in tech got pickier.

Meanwhile, the title "AI Engineer" has become almost meaningless. What hiring managers actually want right now is engineers who can build reliable data pipelines that feed AI systems — not just prompt engineers or fine-tuning specialists. The infrastructure layer is the bottleneck, and candidates who understand both data engineering and ML deployment are commanding serious premiums.

One more thing worth noting: contract and fractional hiring for senior tech roles is up significantly. Companies want flexibility. Senior engineers want autonomy. That alignment is real, and it's reshaping how teams get built.

If you're a hiring manager trying to staff up this year, the talent is there — but your 2022 comp benchmarks won't get you there.

If you're a tech professional weighing your options, the market for specialized skills is stronger than headlines suggest.

We work with both sides of this equation every day. DM us if you want a straight read on what's actually happening in your specific market or role type.

please share resume at Khalil@weradioactive.com


Data Scientist + Data Engineer: ( Expertise in DS and exposure to Azure DE)
Exp- 6–10 years

Position Summary
The role involves developing advanced ML models, building robust data pipelines, and architecting cloud-based data solutions. The incumbent will collaborate with cross-functional teams to deliver high-impact analytical insights and scalable data products.

Key Responsibilities:
Design, develop, and deploy end-to-end ML models and analytical frameworks for business use cases.
Build and maintain scalable ETL/ELT pipelines using modern data engineering tools such as Spark, Airflow, and cloud-native services.
Architect and optimize data lake/data warehouse solutions for performance, reliability, and scalability.
Perform advanced analytics, feature engineering, model tuning, and performance monitoring.
Implement MLOps practices for model versioning, CI/CD, and production monitoring.
Work with structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data to deliver high-quality datasets for analytics.
Collaborate with business stakeholders, product owners, and engineering teams to translate business requirements into technical solutions.
Mentor junior team members and contribute to standardization, best practices, and knowledge sharing.

Requirements / Qualifications:

Education:
Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, Data Science, Engineering, Mathematics, or a related field. Master’s degree preferred.

Experience & Requirements:
6–10 years of hands-on experience across Data Science and Data Engineering domains.
Strong proficiency in Python, SQL, PySpark/Spark, and distributed data processing.
Expertise in ML modelling—classification, regression, clustering, NLP, time series, and model deployment.
Experience building data pipelines and working with tools like Airflow, Kafka, DBT, Hadoop ecosystem.
Proven experience with cloud platforms (AWS/Azure/GCP) and cloud-native data/ML services.
Familiarity with MLOps tools such as MLflow, Kubeflow, SageMaker, or Vertex AI.
Strong communication, analytical thinking, and stakeholder management skills.
Experience working with large-scale datasets and real-time/near-real-time data solutions.
 
Requirements / Qualifications:
Strong problem-solving mindset with the ability to convert business problems into analytical solutions.
Ability to work in a fast-paced, collaborative environment.
Experience with Agile methodologies and cross-functional team collaboration.
Knowledge of BI tools (Power BI, Tableau) is a plus.
Industry experience in BFSI/Retail/Healthcare preferred.

Apply Now
